sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Control flow determines the order in which your code executes. Kotlin improves upon traditional logic structures by treating almost everything as an expression, meaning it can return a value.

sections.heading: Arithmetic & Comparison Operators
sections.content: Kotlin provides standard operators for performing arithmetic operations and logical comparisons.

sections.heading: Equality in Kotlin
sections.content: In Kotlin, == checks structural equality (calls equals()), while === checks referential equality (whether two references point to the same object).

sections.heading: Conditional Expressions: if
sections.content: In Kotlin, if is an expression rather than just a statement. This means it can return a value that can be assigned directly to a variable.

sections.heading: Using if as an Expression
The when Expression
sections.content: The when expression is Kotlin's powerful alternative to the traditional switch statement found in languages like Java or C++.

sections.heading: when Expression Example
sections.code:
val day = 3

val dayName = when (day) {
    1 -> "Monday"
    2 -> "Tuesday"
    3 -> "Wednesday"
    in 4..5 -> "Weekday remainder"
    6, 7 -> "Weekend"
    else -> "Invalid day"
}

sections.heading: Loops
sections.content: Kotlin provides several looping constructs to iterate over ranges, arrays, or collections.

sections.heading: for Loop
sections.content: The for loop works with anything that provides an iterator, such as ranges, arrays, and collections.

sections.heading: for Loop Examples
while and do-while
sections.content: The while and do-while loops behave the same way as they do in most programming languages.

sections.heading: do-while Loop Example
Ranges and Checks
sections.content: Kotlin introduces range operators and the in keyword to make value checks more readable.

sections.heading: Range Checking Example
Summary
items: if and when are expressions and can return values.
when is the preferred alternative to switch statements.
Ranges like .., until, and step simplify iteration.
The in keyword helps check whether a value exists within a range.
